segments.title: Royal Canadian Navy wartime expansion
segments.summary: The lecture explains how Hose, Nelles, trade dependence, escort needs, and destroyer procurement turned a tiny interwar Canadian navy into a major World War II force.
segments.body: The Royal Canadian Navy expanded because Canada needed to exercise sea control for Atlantic trade and convoy protection, while Walter Hose and Percy Nelles preserved the service, built reserve culture, and pushed destroyer procurement despite political and industrial limits. Lawrence escort experience, and Haida/Huron Tribal-class operations.

evidence.note: The opening frames the Royal Canadian Navy's jump from near-disbandment to a large World War II navy built for Atlantic sea control and convoy war.
The trade and reserve-culture section links Canadian economic need, escorting goods, Hose's foresight, and the volunteer reserve's can-do culture.
The Nelles section explains procurement friction, domestic shipbuilding limits, turbine and machinery constraints, and the pressure to prepare before wartime demand.
The Tribal destroyer section shows how long-lead ordering, active production lines, and delayed Canadian approvals shaped wartime fleet growth.
The conclusion restates that Hose and Nelles prepared the force for expansion and that the wartime navy grew to meet a legitimate national need.
